fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

JARRETT J:

This is an application for me to disqualify myself from the further hearing of applications that have been brought by the father of two children following the delivery of reasons for judgment and orders on a final basis in respect of the parenting arrangements for those children. I presided over a trial between the applicant and the respondent with the assistance of an independent children’s lawyer nearly 12 months ago, between 20 and 23 January 2025. I delivered orders and reasons for judgment on 7 August 2025.
The applicant now seeks my disqualification. In support of that application he relies on his affidavit filed on 17 October 2025. The factual basis upon which he seeks disqualification has to do with matters that occurred during the course of the trial. He talks about, first of all, what he describes as a premature direction to settle, something he described, or at least his counsel described to him as, unusual but something which is not unusual, at least in my experience. He talks about some personal accusation that apparently I made against him, with which he does not agree.
Significantly, these matters were not the subject of any application for me to recuse myself at the time of the trial and that of itself carries with it the proposition that because no such application was made, there was nothing in it.
There are some other matters that have occurred since the handing down of the final judgment to which the applicant points. He says that I have not been even-handed in my treatment of he and the other party and the independent children’s lawyer because first, I gave the independent children’s lawyer and the respondent liberty to apply, but not him.
Two things can be said about that. The first is that in the circumstances in which liberty was granted, it was entirely appropriate to limit liberty in that way. Second, irrespective of whether the applicant had liberty to apply or not, he could nonetheless apply, as he has done.
He says that there has been a refusal by me to treat his applications with urgency. Urgency is assessed on the basis of the material that suggests or that is said to suggest the urgency. His application for urgency did not meet the requisite criteria. Simple as that, really.
Further, he complains that on 30 September 2025 I did not make particular orders with respect to parental responsibility that he submitted should be made. That of itself is not surprising, given that there were final orders made in August 2025 and there had been no determination about whether the threshold now required by s 65DAAA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) had been met.
None of these matters pointed to by the applicant satisfy the test that the parties all agreed should be applied in this case. Ebner v Official Trustee in Bankruptcy [2000] HCA 63; (2000) 205 CLR 337 describes the test as follows:
A judge is disqualified if a fair-minded lay observer might reasonably apprehend that the judge might not bring an impartial opinion to the case. A judge has an impartial mind to the resolution to the question which the judge is required to decide. That principle gives effect to the requirement that justice should both be done and seem to be done.
The lay observer is taken to be a reasonable person who understands the duties undertaken by a judicial officer and their oath. In Ebner, the High Court set out the relevant way in which the test works. At [8] the members of the Court said this:
The apprehension of bias principle admits of the possibility of human frailty. Its application is as diverse as human frailty. Its application requires two steps. First, it requires the identification of what is said might lead a judge or juror to decide a case other than on its legal and factual merits. The second step is no less important; there must be an articulation of the logical connection between the matter and the feared deviation from the course of deciding the case on its merits. The bare assertion that a judge or a juror has an interest in litigation or an interest in a party to it will be of no assistance unless the nature of the interest and the asserted connection with the possibility of departure from impartial decision-making is articulated. Only then can the reasonableness of the asserted apprehension of bias be assessed.
The onus on the applicant is a heavy one. He needs to discharge that onus to secure an order that I should not sit any further on any applications in this case.
The matters to which he has referred in his affidavit do not satisfy the tests I have referred to at [8] and [9] above. Whilst he has identified particular matters about which he complains, there has not been any articulation of a logical connection between those matters and:
... the feared deviation from the course of deciding the case on its merits.
To that extent, the applicant’s case for disqualification fails.
But that is not the end of the matter. I raised with counsel for the respondent the proposition that I had made credit findings against the applicant in the course of my reasons for judgment. It is apparent from my reasons for judgment that I took the view that the evidence established substantial concerns regarding the applicant’s credit. I said that he gave patently inaccurate evidence about the status of his employment and took no steps to correct that incorrect or inaccurate evidence. My reasons for judgment demonstrate that the applicant was shown throughout the evidence to be a person who makes serious threats, including threats to kill others, consistently with contemporaneous text messages that were tendered in evidence, and with his presentation in the witness box.
I found that his behaviour was reflective of a dogmatic, unchangeable, and manipulative tendency – see for example paragraph [29]. I found that some of the applicant’s evidence was misleading, contrived, and unreliable – see for example his evidence about school changeovers at paragraphs [64] and [65]. I found that his evidence about various matters, including decisions concerning the children’s medical and therapeutic care, were evasive and incomplete – see for example [64] and [65]. I had a particularly important concern that arose from the applicant’s evidence about the children’s therapeutic treatment and NDIS funding. His explanations for the NDIS funding and the cessation of treatment and the like, as I recorded in my reasons at [77] and [78], revealed an appalling disregard for one of the children’s therapeutic needs and demonstrated him to prioritise financial considerations over their necessary support. To the extent that the applicant gave evidence that he was relying upon the advice of others when he took the steps about which I was critical, I formed the view that he gave evidence which was false or misleading to justify his conduct: see paragraph [78].
I also found that the applicant’s evidence, which dealt with purported advice from a child psychiatrist, was fabricated. That is a direct finding of dishonesty. I also found that he had failed to comply with court-ordered family therapy, despite a clear obligation to facilitate it: see paragraphs [88], [89] to [90].
Overall, I took the view that the applicant’s conduct demonstrated a consistent pattern of non-compliance, manipulation, unreliability and a failure to give truthful, complete or candid evidence. I expressed the view that I was utterly persuaded that the applicant will not abide by the terms of any orders of the Court and that his credit was significantly compromised. What has occurred since the delivery of those reasons bears out those predictions set out in paragraph [147] of the reasons.
In Jess & Jess [2021] FamCAFC 159, the Full Court of the Family Court of Australia at 396 said this:
As we have discussed in addressing grounds 1 to 25 of this appeal, the 2018 reasons contain numerous seriously adverse findings to the credit and credibility of each of Mr Jess Junior, Mr Ban and Ms C. Jess. Those findings are expressed in unqualified terms that almost inevitably gives rise to a reasonable apprehension of bias. As was observed by the New South Wales Court of Appeal in Australian National Industries v Spedley Securities Limited [1992] 26 NSWLR 411 at 442, that a previous decision of the same fact or upon the credibility of a relevant witness will create such an apprehension normally, if not inevitably follows.
The passage from Jess & Jess that I have just quoted echoes what was previously said in R v Watson; Ex parte Armstrong [1976] HCA 39; [1976] 136, CLR 248 at 265 to 266, and subsequently applied in numerous decisions of this court, the Full Court of the Federal Court of Australia, and single judge decisions of the Federal Court of Australia and this Court. In Bob Jane Corporation Proprietary Limited v ACN 149 801 141 Proprietary Limited [2015] FCA 1343, Besanko J considered this aspect of the apprehended bias rule in some detail. I do not intend to set out Besanko J’s reasons in a lengthy way, save to say that between paragraph [68] and [70] his Honour reiterated the proposition that:
Where there are findings of credit relied upon by an applicant for disqualification, the Court will ordinarily accede to such a request.
So much also appears, by way of example, from the judgment of R. Derrington J in Australian Securities and Investments Commission v Sunshine Loans Proprietary Limited (No 3) [2024] FCA 786 at paragraph 14. As R Derrington J points out there, however, much will depend upon the circumstances, and there really is no general rule available to resolve every matter but:
In the ordinary course where a judge has made a finding as to the credit of a witness who will then subsequently give contentious evidence, it is difficult to imagine a circumstance in which that judge may proceed to hear the matter.
Perhaps most recently, in an appeal from that decision in Australian Securities and Investments Commission v Sunshine Loans Proprietary Limited [2025] FCAFC 32; [2025] 308 FCR 514, the Full Court of the Federal Court of Australia said that the rule is not so much a principle that needs to be followed in every case, but it will depend upon the facts of each case for its application.
Here, the findings that I made against the applicant in my reasons for judgment are serious. I found that he was untruthful and, on one occasion, dishonest. The findings I made were unqualified. There was no particular qualification attached to any of them and, in those circumstances, those findings are significant.
The matters to which I have just referred arising out of my reasons for judgment were not raised on this application by the applicant. They have been raised by me. I have given counsel for the respondent and the independent children’s lawyer the opportunity to address me on those issues but, it seems to me, notwithstanding that the applicant did not raise them directly or even indirectly, they are nonetheless matters which I need to take into account because, as the High Court says in Ebner, justice not only needs to be done, it needs to be seen to be done.
In those circumstances, it seems to me that a reasonable lay observer might reasonably apprehend that I would not bring an open mind to any further determinations of contentious evidence between these two parties in any subsequent application. That there will be contentious evidence between these parties in subsequent applications is something which is, in my view, a certainty. Whilst Ms Lindsay of counsel for the respondent, pointed out that on an application under s 65DAAA, if s 65DAAA permits of standalone applications (something of which I am not entirely satisfied) but if that was to be dealt with on its own, then I would need to take the applicant’s evidence at its highest. That is to say, I would have to accept what he says.
That might be so, but that is only part of the question because once a Court is satisfied that there has been a significant or material change in circumstances, having regard to the evidence of the applicant for such a finding, the Court must then go on to determine what orders, if any, should be made in the best interest of the child or children concerned. It is at that point that there will be questions of contested testimony, and at that point where real questions of apprehension of bias will arise. The applications before me roll them all up into one.
The applicant seeks orders that vary, in significant respects, the final orders that I pronounced in August 2025. The respondent says that the threshold established by s 65DAAA has not been reached. Without deciding one way or another, I record that if she is wrong about that, then further considerations will inevitably follow because the applicant’s application asks for a reconsideration of the August 2025 orders.
In all of those circumstances, I consider that the onus on the applicant in this application has been discharged, and I disqualify myself from any further participation in any applications between these parties. The application will be referred to another judge or judicial officer, and when that has occurred, the parties will be informed. I will make a request for priority.
